grounding_source_rows: human_disagreements rows 28, 352; taxonomy Workers' Comp subcategory descriptions
grounding_note: Workers' comp routes by SYSTEM: state comp, the separate federal system (e.g., postal workers), longshore/harbor (LHWA), maritime (Jones Act, which is General Litigation > Maritime), and stress/psychological claims. The employer's identity and the injury's nature — facts every worker knows — select the system. Row 352 ('worker's compensation, worker's rights') shows even humans can't route without them.

scenarios.opening_query: I was hurt on the job and my claim keeps getting bounced between offices with no answers.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > State || Workers' Comp > Federal
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm a mail carrier — USPS — so it's the federal workers' comp system, not the state one.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I'm a USPS mail carrier — it's the federal comp system, not state.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the employer is the federal government
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I work for a private delivery company — regular state comp.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I'm with a private delivery company — regular state comp.
scenarios.vetting_note: The taxonomy's own federal example (postal worker); employer identity is pure routing.

scenarios.opening_query: My workers' comp claim was denied and my employer's insurer says I can't prove it happened at work.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > State || Workers' Comp > Stress Related
scenarios.hidden_fact: The claim is for PTSD — I'm a 911 dispatcher and took the call for a mass-casualty crash; there's no physical injury, which is why they're fighting it.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a PTSD claim — I'm a 911 dispatcher and took a mass-casualty call. No physical injury, which is why they fight it.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the claim is psychological rather than physical
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's a torn rotator cuff from lifting a patient — a straightforward physical injury claim.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my torn rotator cuff from lifting a patient — a physical injury.
scenarios.vetting_note: Stress claims have their own label and much harder proof rules; injury type is the fact.

scenarios.opening_query: I got badly injured working on the waterfront and I don't know which system my claim belongs in.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > State || Workers' Comp > LHWA
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm a longshoreman — I was loading container ships on the dock when the rigging failed.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I'm a longshoreman — loading container ships on the dock when the rigging failed.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the work is longshore/harbor labor covered by LHWA
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I work in the port district's landside warehouse — ordinary state-covered warehouse work.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I'm in the landside warehouse — ordinary warehouse work.
scenarios.vetting_note: LHWA is a distinct federal scheme with its own label; dock-vs-warehouse is the jurisdictional line.

scenarios.opening_query: I was injured during the season and the company is stalling on everything while I can't work.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > State || General Litigation > Maritime
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm a deckhand — it happened at sea on a commercial fishing vessel, and I'm told that means maritime law, not regular comp.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I'm a deckhand — it happened at sea on a commercial fishing boat, which apparently means maritime law.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the injury occurred at sea as a seaman
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I work in the cannery on shore — the injury happened on the processing line.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I'm in the shore cannery — it happened on the processing line.
scenarios.vetting_note: Jones Act seamen are excluded from comp entirely; at-sea status reroutes to Maritime.

scenarios.opening_query: My comp checks stopped and then my boss let me go while I was still on restrictions.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > State || Labor & Employment > Wrongful Discharge - Employee
scenarios.hidden_fact: The firing came three days after my comp claim was accepted — HR even mentioned the claim in the meeting. I want to pursue the retaliation, not just the checks.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: They fired me three days after the claim was accepted and mentioned it in the meeting — I want the retaliation pursued, not just the checks.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether comp-claim retaliation is the primary grievance
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The 'firing' was a misunderstanding — I'm still employed; the real problem is the stopped time-loss checks.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The firing was a mix-up — I'm still employed. The problem is the stopped checks.
scenarios.vetting_note: Comp retaliation is the taxonomy's own Wrongful Discharge example; the timing fact links them.

scenarios.opening_query: I need help with workers' compensation and my rights at my job. Things have gotten bad.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Workers' Comp > State || Labor & Employment > General - Employee
scenarios.hidden_fact: There's no injury or claim — 'comp' was the word I grabbed. My real problems are cut hours, denied breaks, and a supervisor who screams at us.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: There's no injury — I just grabbed the word 'comp.' It's cut hours, denied breaks, and a screaming supervisor.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an actual work injury underlies the request
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: There IS an injury — my back went out on shift in March and the claim was never filed properly.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: There is an injury — my back went out on shift in March and the claim never got filed right.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 352 verbatim: users conflate 'comp' with workplace rights; the injury-existence question routes it.
